critical_issues: Table count claim '57 (43 core + 14 future-ready)' is incorrect. Schema DDL defines 49 core tables (Sections 2.1–2.9) and 7 future-ready tables (Section 2.10) = 56 total. The manifest also lists only 56 distinct table entries. The 43+14 split and the 57 total are arithmetically wrong, and downstream phases will consume this count from the manifest.

ERD (Section 1.2) shows course_notes, course_flags, vendor_notes, and vendor_flags as distinct tables with 1:N relationships, but no SQL DDL is provided for any of these four tables in Section 2. D3 F-025 explicitly requires 'Notes and flags as separate linked entities per the same pattern as client reputation (F-016)' for courses, and D3 F-029 requires the same for vendors. These tables are bound by D55-P2-008 and D55-P2-024 but are missing from the actual schema.

course_portal_users table is referenced in the Future-Proofing Manifest (Section 6) as a planned empty table for F-083, and D2 D55-P2-028 explicitly binds Phase 4 to 'accommodate the future possibility of a Course Contact user type.' The table is never defined in Section 2.10's future-ready SQL.

Data Dictionary (Section 5.1) covers approximately 15 entities out of 56 tables. Over 35 tables—including client_relationships, group_members, delegates, proposals, proposal_options, round_participants, refunds, commission_invoice_line_items, escalations, incidents, and all future-ready tables—have no dictionary entries. A Phase 4 Data Dictionary should document every table.
contradictions: D2 D55-P2-015 binds Phase 4 to 'implement tenant isolation via operator_id on all data tables with RLS enforcement.' D2 glossary for Multi-Tenancy states 'operator_id foreign key on all data tables.' D3 D55-P3-003 requires 'All entities include operator_id scoping.' D4 uses 'organization_id' on all tables and names the anchor table 'organizations' instead of 'operators.' The concept is identical but the column name contradicts the explicit binding from three prior decision ledger entries.

D3 F-009 data implications state 'Creates group_member records linked to booking and optionally to client (for the trip organizer relationship).' D2 Appendix B glossary entry for Group Member specifies 'group_member entity with foreign key to booking and client (trip organizer).' D4's group_members table has booking_id but no client_id FK for the trip organizer link.

D3 F-066 data implications specify 'branding_config record per operator with logo file reference, primary/secondary brand colors, font preferences, and any custom text (tagline, footer).' D4 inlines these fields directly on the organizations table instead of a separate branding_config entity. While functionally equivalent, it contradicts D3's stated entity design.

RLS Section 3.4 summary table classifies 'destinations' as USER-FACING with SELECT for 'all roles,' but Section 3.2 states the universal tenant isolation policy (which checks organization_id) is 'applied to every USER-FACING table.' The destinations table has no organization_id column, making this policy inapplicable and the 'every USER-FACING table' claim incorrect for destinations.
warnings: RLS coverage claim '57/57 tables — 100%' inherits the incorrect table count. The actual coverage should reference the correct number of tables, and the four missing schema tables (course_notes, course_flags, vendor_notes, vendor_flags) have no RLS policies because they don't exist.

D3 F-076 (Data Archival, Expansion tier) specifies 'archived boolean flag on all major entities.' D4 uses deleted_at (soft delete) but provides no separate archived concept. D3 explicitly distinguishes archival from deletion—archived records are 'hidden from active views but fully searchable,' while soft delete implies a different semantic. No acknowledgment of this design choice or deviation.

All 4 of 4 research points (4.1–4.4) were not executed as noted in Appendix D. While transparently disclosed, this means the property/course separation pattern, Supabase RLS syntax, generated tsvector column support, and regulatory updates are entirely unvalidated. Phase 5 carries significant verification burden.

Explicit index count in the manifest is stated as '32 (explicit)' but a line-by-line count of Section 4.2 and 4.3 yields 31 (28 in high-traffic + 3 full-text search). Minor arithmetic error but could mislead Phase 5.

The availability_requests table links to booking_rounds (FK booking_round_id) whereas D3 F-007 describes availability tracking at the booking level ('Track the status of tee time requests sent to courses for each booking'). The round-level association is arguably better architecture but the shift from booking-level to round-level is not explicitly justified or noted as a refinement from D3.

Four JSONB fields (clients.preferences_metadata, vendors.rate_structure, proposal_options.proposed_rounds, escalations.context_snapshot) lack documented key schemas. Risk 4.2 flags this but defers all mitigation to Phase 5. No example schemas or validation rules are provided in D4 despite the Data Dictionary being the natural home for this.

The team_members.email column has a UNIQUE constraint without organization_id scope, meaning the same email cannot exist across different tenants. This may block multi-operator scenarios where the same person (e.g., Rafael as both Operator and Platform Owner) needs accounts in multiple organizations.

Section 6 Future-Proofing Manifest lists 17 elements but mixes table-level items with column-level items (e.g., clients.membership_status is a column, not a table). The label 'Total future-proofing elements: 17 tables/columns' could confuse downstream phases expecting only table-level additions.
